conclusion: All frozen atomic expected IDs are sensible authoritative anchors. Exact-ID coverage is a strict identity metric, while important-negative ID assertions measure anchor presence and require separate semantic review of the stated contrast or exclusion.

metric_interpretation.atomic_answer_completeness: Requires every frozen canonical claim ID. It is intentionally stricter than semantic correctness and can under-credit a correct answer supported by a different valid record.
metric_interpretation.important_negative_coverage: Requires at least one listed evidence anchor for each negative. It does not prove that the response actually states the required negative; the blinded semantic review checks that.
metric_interpretation.evidence_validity: Checks that emitted IDs use the closed authoritative binding, concept path, paper ID, source path, and declared locator set.

questions.question: A production router must choose among question-only or standalone-model answering, basic RAG, and GraphRAG before generation. Derive an evidence-based decision boundary for simple facts, interconnected synthesis, and noisy graph evidence, and explain why an always-use-the-graph policy is unsupported.

atomic_answers.statement: For simple fact retrieval, basic RAG can be comparable to or better than GraphRAG because graph expansion can add noise.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: GraphRAG has its clearest reported advantages on complex reasoning, contextual summarization, and creative generation.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: Supplying an entire noisy textual graph can perform worse than answering from the question alone.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: Graph retrieval and standalone model reasoning each correct cases missed by the other, so neither is a universally dominant answer source.
atomic_answers.review_note: The record reports correction in both directions. The conclusion that neither route is universally dominant is a bounded contrast rather than a literal sentence in the record.

important_negatives.statement: Do not claim that adding graph context is always beneficial or that GraphRAG should replace every non-graph route.
important_negatives.review_note: The listed IDs are sensible evidence anchors for the explicit join, contrast, conditional, or exclusion. The negative is not asserted to be a literal sentence in every individual record.

derivation.conclusion: Route simple direct lookups toward basic retrieval, but prefer graph machinery when the task actually requires interconnected synthesis.
Graph use must also be gated on evidence quality and should preserve a non-graph fallback when retrieval is noisy or unhelpful.

bindings.authoritative_text: Inference-only results reveal that adding an entire noisy textual graph can underperform answering from the question alone.

Author-reported result: In the preliminary categorization, 45.64% of previously incorrect responses were rectified by GraphRAG, while 16.89% of samples fell in the category where the standalone LLM outperformed GraphRAG.
Author-reported result: Basic RAG is comparable to or better than GraphRAG on simple fact retrieval, where graph expansion can add noise.
Author-reported result: GraphRAG methods show their clearest advantages on complex reasoning, contextual summarization, and creative generation.

questions.question: Classify the corpus evidence about changing knowledge into explicit add-only behavior, architectural update claims, and unimplemented or unspecified lifecycle work. Which systems support each class, and why does none of that evidence establish a complete deletion, provenance-repair, and re-indexing protocol?

atomic_answers.statement: LightRAG specifies an add-only update path that processes new documents and merges their extracted nodes and edges into the existing graph.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: HippoRAG argues that new knowledge can be added as graph associations instead of recomputing a summary hierarchy.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: CommunityKG-RAG claims that precomputed structures permit rapid updates but does not provide a detailed maintenance algorithm or demonstrated protocol.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: MedGraphRAG leaves real-time updates as future work and neither implements nor measures them.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: The practical dependency-based framework motivates refreshes but does not specify deletion, provenance repair, or re-indexing behavior.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

important_negatives.statement: Do not infer full update correctness, deletion support, or provenance repair from an add-only merge or a claim of rapid updates.
important_negatives.review_note: The listed IDs are sensible evidence anchors for the explicit join, contrast, conditional, or exclusion. The negative is not asserted to be a literal sentence in every individual record.

derivation.conclusion: The strongest positive evidence concerns incremental addition, not a complete mutable-index lifecycle.
A specified add-only algorithm must be distinguished from an architectural claim and from explicitly deferred work.
None of the positive add-only evidence establishes deletion, source replacement, provenance repair, or consistency under concurrent change.

bindings.authoritative_text: The authors contrast incremental edge addition in HippoRAG with summary-based indexes that must be recomputed when new data arrives.
Real-time updates are not implemented or measured; they are explicitly identified as future work.

The paper says precomputed structures support rapid updates, but does not specify a detailed incremental graph-maintenance algorithm; this is an author claim rather than a demonstrated update protocol.
Incremental updates process only new documents and merge new nodes and edges into the existing graph.
The paper motivates dynamic-content refresh but does not specify a concrete incremental deletion, provenance, or re-indexing protocol.

questions.question: A graph index can fail through random edge deletion, injected irrelevant or incorrect paths, or wrong and incomplete extracted triples. Match each corruption type to the strongest defense actually supported by the corpus, and explain why the three robustness claims are not interchangeable.

atomic_answers.statement: PathRAG retained a reported advantage over LightRAG and NaiveRAG through 50 percent random edge removal, despite degradation from graph sparsification.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: GraphRAG-FI's filtering recovered part of the performance lost after 30 irrelevant and incorrect paths were injected.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: KAG's graph-only logical-form route is faster but can lose accuracy when extracted subject-predicate-object facts are wrong or incomplete.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: KAG uses original chunks to supplement structured results when graph facts omit detail or generation context.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

important_negatives.statement: Do not present any one result as proof of robustness to every graph defect or as evidence of zero degradation.
important_negatives.review_note: The listed IDs are sensible evidence anchors for the explicit join, contrast, conditional, or exclusion. The negative is not asserted to be a literal sentence in every individual record.

derivation.conclusion: Use path robustness for missing edges, filtering for injected candidates, and a text-backed hybrid route for incorrect or incomplete extracted facts.
Robustness to deletion and robustness to added noise are different interventions and cannot be inferred from one another.

bindings.authoritative_text: Structured graph results can be returned directly, while original chunks supplement missing detail and generation context.
Graph-only logical-form reasoning is faster than hybrid retrieval but can lose accuracy when extracted SPO facts are wrong or incomplete.
Graph sparsification degrades graph-based systems, but PathRAG maintained a reported advantage over LightRAG and NaiveRAG through 50% random edge removal.
Author-reported result: After adding 30 noisy paths, filtering improved CWQ Hit by 2.23% and F1 by 3.63% over the noisy RoG condition.

questions.question: Use four distinct ablations to refute the rule that retrieval quality must improve whenever a system increases candidates, chunk size, sentence coverage, or context-window size. What trade-off recurs, and which reported optima must remain system-specific?

atomic_answers.statement: G-Retriever must balance too few semantic candidates, which can miss evidence, against too many candidates, which add distracting graph content.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: GraphReader reports empirical optima at five initial nodes and 2k-token chunks; more nodes add noise and both larger and smaller chunks can lose information.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: CommunityKG-RAG reports non-monotonic accuracy as sentence inclusion changes at a fixed community threshold.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: ROGRAG reports lower accuracy at a 64k maximum context than at 32k, attributing the decline to harder extraction of subtle information.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

important_negatives.statement: Do not generalize any reported candidate, chunk, threshold, or context optimum to every corpus and model.
important_negatives.review_note: The listed IDs are sensible evidence anchors for the explicit join, contrast, conditional, or exclusion. The negative is not asserted to be a literal sentence in every individual record.

derivation.conclusion: Candidate count, chunk size, sentence coverage, and model context length all exhibit evidence-recall versus distraction or extraction trade-offs.
The evidence rejects a monotonic more-context-is-better rule but does not establish one universal optimum across systems.

bindings.authoritative_text: The number of initial semantic candidates must balance missing evidence against distracting graph content.
Five initial nodes and 2k-token chunks are empirical optima; more nodes add noise and either much larger or smaller chunks lose information.
At a fixed 25% community threshold, sentence inclusion produced non-monotonic accuracy, indicating a context-versus-noise tradeoff.
Increasing maximum context from 32k to 64k reduced accuracy, which the authors associate with harder extraction of subtle information.

questions.question: Design a minimal evidence-organization contract that must support both relational reasoning and citation-level detail. Which ablations or limitations show that a learned structural token, a flat fact list, a path summary, or a community summary cannot safely serve as the only representation?

atomic_answers.statement: G-Retriever's learned structural graph token and explicit textualized graph provide complementary information.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: PathRAG's path-structured prompts outperform flattened prompts, supporting preservation of within-path dependencies.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: KAG combines structured graph results with original chunks so missing detail and generation context can be recovered.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: Community summaries can lose specific examples, quotations, and citations that matter to answer quality.
atomic_answers.review_note: The reviewed record identifies examples, quotations, and citations as details graph extraction should retain better. Framing that as possible loss from high-level community summaries is a cautious synthesis.

important_negatives.statement: Do not claim that structure alone, flattened facts alone, or high-level summaries alone preserve every form of answer evidence.
important_negatives.review_note: The listed IDs are sensible evidence anchors for the explicit join, contrast, conditional, or exclusion. The negative is not asserted to be a literal sentence in every individual record.

derivation.conclusion: A loss-aware prompt contract should preserve relational order or structure while retaining source text for detail and generation context.
A thematic summary alone is insufficient when the answer requires local quotations, examples, or citations.

bindings.authoritative_text: Ablations support complementarity between the GNN graph token and explicit textualized graph.
Empowerment results are mixed; evaluator analysis suggests that specific examples, quotations, and citations are important details that graph extraction may need to retain better.
Structured graph results can be returned directly, while original chunks supplement missing detail and generation context.
Path-structured prompts outperformed flattened prompts, supporting preservation of within-path dependencies.

questions.question: Specify an evaluation protocol that can tell retrieval success from parametric-knowledge success. It must address pipeline-stage metrics, open versus grounding-required prompts, questionable reference answers, and LLM-judge dependence; explain the failure each safeguard prevents.

atomic_answers.statement: Evaluation should measure graph quality, retrieval recall and relevance, and generation accuracy, faithfulness, and coverage as separate stages.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: Permissive prompts can hide retrieval failure because the model can answer from parametric knowledge.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: Open mode measures end-task capability with parametric knowledge, whereas reject mode requires retrieval-grounded answering or abstention.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: A contamination-aware check found direct model answers that exceeded an UltraDomain ground truth, so evaluation should target domains where the base model is weak and verify the reference answer.
atomic_answers.review_note: The record directly supports the contamination concern. Independently verifying a reference answer is the benchmark-design consequence drawn from that observation.

atomic_answers.statement: When high-level questions lack direct ground truth, reliance on an LLM pairwise judge creates an acknowledged evaluation-scope limitation.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

important_negatives.statement: Do not treat answer accuracy under a permissive prompt as proof that retrieval succeeded.
important_negatives.review_note: The listed IDs are sensible evidence anchors for the explicit join, contrast, conditional, or exclusion. The negative is not asserted to be a literal sentence in every individual record.

important_negatives.statement: Do not treat an LLM judge or an unverified benchmark answer as an unquestionable ground truth.
important_negatives.review_note: The listed IDs are sensible evidence anchors for the explicit join, contrast, conditional, or exclusion. The negative is not asserted to be a literal sentence in every individual record.

derivation.conclusion: A defensible benchmark must report retrieval separately and include a grounding-required condition that exposes unsupported answers.
Reference answers and judge-based assessments need independent checks, especially when parametric knowledge or missing direct ground truth can distort conclusions.

bindings.authoritative_text: Because complex high-level questions lack ground-truth answers, the main evaluation uses GPT-4o-mini as a pairwise judge, creating an evaluation-scope limitation explicitly acknowledged by the authors.
Preliminary contamination-aware analysis showing that direct LLM answers can outperform UltraDomain ground truth, motivating domain-specific evaluation where the base model is weak.
Evaluation is stage-specific: graph quality, retrieval recall/relevance, and generation accuracy/faithfulness/coverage are assessed separately.
GraphRAG evaluation is prompt-sensitive, and permissive prompts can hide retrieval failure through parametric knowledge leakage.
Author claim: Open and reject evaluation modes respectively measure end-task capability with parametric knowledge and retrieval-grounded capability with required abstention.

questions.question: For a changing professional corpus with a limited construction budget, implicit relations, and domain shift, derive a staged graph-construction policy. When should dependency extraction, LLM or open extraction, and schema-constrained work be used, and what must stay aligned with query-time retrieval?

atomic_answers.statement: Dependency-based and LLM-based extraction can be exposed as interchangeable construction modes to make the cost-accuracy trade-off explicit.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: Surface-syntax dependency extraction can miss implicit and context-dependent relations.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: Open information extraction has alignment and consistency problems, while rigorous schema-constrained extraction requires expensive expert labor.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: A shared graph schema can constrain extraction and provide the vocabulary for query decomposition.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: Optimizing construction or retrieval in isolation can underperform under domain shift because the two stages are interdependent.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

important_negatives.statement: Do not claim that dependency parsing captures implicit relations, that open extraction is automatically consistent, or that schema rigor is free.
important_negatives.review_note: The listed IDs are sensible evidence anchors for the explicit join, contrast, conditional, or exclusion. The negative is not asserted to be a literal sentence in every individual record.

important_negatives.statement: Do not optimize the index without checking that query decomposition can address the resulting graph vocabulary.
important_negatives.review_note: The listed IDs are sensible evidence anchors for the explicit join, contrast, conditional, or exclusion. The negative is not asserted to be a literal sentence in every individual record.

derivation.conclusion: Use inexpensive dependency extraction for explicit relations, but escalate ambiguous or implicit cases to LLM or schema-aware extraction while budgeting expert schema work.
Construction decisions must remain aligned with the vocabulary and routes used at query time, particularly under domain shift.

bindings.authoritative_text: Open information extraction creates alignment and consistency problems, while rigorous schema-constrained construction requires expensive expert labor.
Author claim: The framework exposes dependency-based and LLM-based extraction as interchangeable modes to support an explicit cost-accuracy trade-off.
Author-acknowledged limitation: Surface-syntax dependency extraction can miss implicit and context-dependent relations.
Author claim: Construction-only and retrieval-only GraphRAG optimizations neglect their interdependence and can underperform under domain shift.
Author claim: A shared graph schema bounds extraction and supplies the vocabulary used by the query decomposer.

questions.question: Construct a failure-aware query router for detailed entity questions, abstract thematic questions, partially specified triples, and complex decomposable questions. State the initial route for each case and the fallback when structured decomposition or verification fails.

atomic_answers.statement: LightRAG routes detailed queries through entity keys and abstract queries through relation or theme keys.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: PolyG selects traversal behavior from which subject, predicate, or object components are missing instead of applying one policy to every graph question.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: ROGRAG attempts structured decomposition first and falls back to fuzzy dual-level matching when decomposition or verification fails.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: Youtu-GraphRAG decomposes complex questions into schema-valid atomic subqueries and executes entity, triple, community, and bounded path routes.
atomic_answers.review_note: The reviewed interpretation names DFS traversal; its cited paper page specifies a maximum depth of five, so the word 'bounded' is supported by the locator rather than repeated in the interpretation.

important_negatives.statement: Do not collapse entity/detail routing, missing-triple traversal, schema decomposition, and failure fallback into one undifferentiated graph search.
important_negatives.review_note: The listed IDs are sensible evidence anchors for the explicit join, contrast, conditional, or exclusion. The negative is not asserted to be a literal sentence in every individual record.

derivation.conclusion: The initial route should depend on scope, known versus missing graph components, and whether the question can be decomposed into schema-valid atomic work.
A structured route needs an explicit fuzzy fallback when decomposition or verification is unsuccessful.

bindings.authoritative_text: Synthesis: LightRAG's semantic distinction is operational rather than ontological—entity keys route detailed queries, relation/theme keys route abstract queries, and both return profiled text for one generator.
The system attempts structured decomposition first and degrades to fuzzy dual-level matching when decomposition or verification is unsuccessful.
PolyG adapts traversal to a question's missing triple components instead of using one traversal policy for all questions.
Author claim: The retrieval agent decomposes complex questions into schema-valid atomic sub-queries and executes entity, triple, community, and DFS routes.

questions.question: Reconcile the corpus's strongest online-efficiency claims without turning incomparable numbers into a league table. For GraphRAG reports, LightRAG, HippoRAG, and GraphReader, identify the mechanism, exact comparison baseline, and any quality trade-off that must travel with the cost claim.

atomic_answers.statement: GraphRAG root-level reports use more than 97 percent fewer query-time context tokens than raw-text map-reduce while retaining an advantage over vector RAG in the reported setup.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: The same GraphRAG study reports only small improvements of community reports over raw-text map-reduce and a root-level quality-versus-token trade-off.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: LightRAG reports fewer than 100 retrieval-keyword tokens and one API call against GraphRAG in its Legal cost analysis by avoiding per-community traversal.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: HippoRAG reports single-step online retrieval as 10 to 30 times cheaper and 6 to 13 times faster than IRCoT while remaining competitive.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: GraphReader accepts modestly higher token use than ReadAgent in the 256k analysis in exchange for substantially stronger task scores.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

important_negatives.statement: Do not rank the systems directly by the reported percentages or ratios because the studies use different tasks, baselines, models, and cost definitions.
important_negatives.review_note: The listed IDs are sensible evidence anchors for the explicit join, contrast, conditional, or exclusion. The negative is not asserted to be a literal sentence in every individual record.

derivation.conclusion: Even within one study, lower token cost does not imply an unqualified quality win.
The papers reduce online work through different mechanisms and compare against different baselines, so their ratios form separate baseline-bound claims rather than a cross-paper speed ranking.

bindings.authoritative_text: Root-level reports retain advantages over vector RAG while requiring more than 97 percent fewer query-time context tokens than raw-text map-reduce.
Graph community reports generally provide only small improvements over raw-text map-reduce, and root-level reports trade some quality for much lower token cost.
The authors report single-step online retrieval as 10 to 30 times cheaper and 6 to 13 times faster than IRCoT while remaining competitive.
GraphReader uses only modestly more tokens than ReadAgent in the reported 256k analysis but achieves much stronger task scores.
Against GraphRAG, LightRAG avoids per-community traversal and reported fewer than 100 retrieval-keyword tokens and one API call in the Legal cost analysis.

questions.question: Define two answer-source policies: one for open answering that may combine retrieved and parametric knowledge, and one for retrieval-grounded answering that must abstain when evidence is insufficient. Where should support checking and confidence gating occur, and how should fact verification represent insufficient evidence?

atomic_answers.statement: GraphRAG-Integration applies separate confidence thresholds to retrieved-graph and standalone-model answers before combining retained candidates.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: ROGRAG reports that verifying whether context supports the query before generation outperforms checking the completed answer afterward.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: CommunityKG-RAG's fact-verification contract includes supported, refuted, and not-enough-information outcomes.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

atomic_answers.statement: Open evaluation permits parametric knowledge, while reject evaluation requires retrieval-grounded capability and abstention when evidence is insufficient.
atomic_answers.review_note: The atomic statement is a faithful paraphrase of the reviewed interpretation.

important_negatives.statement: Do not silently use parametric knowledge in a retrieval-grounded or reject-mode answer when the retrieved evidence is insufficient.
important_negatives.review_note: The listed IDs are sensible evidence anchors for the explicit join, contrast, conditional, or exclusion. The negative is not asserted to be a literal sentence in every individual record.

important_negatives.statement: Do not conflate pre-generation evidence support, candidate confidence fusion, and post-generation answer checking.
important_negatives.review_note: The listed IDs are sensible evidence anchors for the explicit join, contrast, conditional, or exclusion. The negative is not asserted to be a literal sentence in every individual record.

derivation.conclusion: An open-answer policy can gate graph and parametric candidates independently and test evidence support before generation.
When the contract requires grounding, unsupported evidence must produce an explicit not-enough-information or abstention outcome rather than a parametric guess.

bindings.authoritative_text: Three-way claim verification: supported, refuted, or not enough information.
Checking whether context supports the query before generation outperformed checking the completed response afterward.
Author claim: GraphRAG-Integration independently applies confidence thresholds to GraphRAG and standalone-LLM answers, then combines their retained candidates.
Author claim: Open and reject evaluation modes respectively measure end-task capability with parametric knowledge and retrieval-grounded capability with required abstention.
